metadata.title: The Secret Lives of Numbers: A Hidden History of Math — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The authors challenge foundational myths, showing a single Western origin for math is imperialism, not truth. Multiplying by zero gives zero, but zero's history as a placeholder is older and non-Western. Aryabhata used the word kha to mark empty positions long before the modern symbol. Inventions like the German balance wheel of 1510 and 16th-century sand glasses show technology's tangled global roots.

artifactLines: The authors challenge foundational myths, showing a single Western origin for math is imperialism, not truth.
Multiplying by zero gives zero, but zero's history as a placeholder is older and non-Western.
Aryabhata used the word kha to mark empty positions long before the modern symbol.
Inventions like the German balance wheel of 1510 and 16th-century sand glasses show technology's tangled global roots.

beats.narration_text: The claim that the myth of a lone Western origin for math is imperialism rather than mathematical truth reframed the whole history for me, especially Aryabhata using kha for empty positions. I want to trace one concrete thread. Take the story of zero and (1) contrast Aryabhata's kha with the later symbol we use, (2) connect an invention like the German balance wheel of 1510 to the sand glasses of 16th-century voyages, and (3) name one foundational myth the authors overturn.
